heroOverview: Tritanomaly is a rare inherited deficiency of short-wavelength (blue) cone photoreceptors in which the cone is still present but contains an abnormal photopigment, reducing sensitivity to blue and yellow hues. Unlike the far more common red-green color deficiencies, tritanomaly affects males and females equally, as the underlying gene — OPN1SW — is located on chromosome 7 rather than the X chromosome. Prevalence estimates range from 0.0001% to 0.002% of the population, making it the least common form of congenital color vision deficiency. Affected individuals confuse blue with green and yellow with violet or grey, but retain far more color discrimination than people with total blue-cone absence (tritanopia). Acquired tritanomaly, caused by retinal disease, glaucoma, or drug toxicity, is considerably more common than the congenital form.

keyStats.prevalence: Congenital tritanomaly: estimated 0.0001-0.002% of the general population; acquired forms are more common and associated with retinal or optic nerve disease
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally (autosomal inheritance); acquired form more frequent after age 50 with retinal degeneration
keyStats.avgAge: Congenital form present from birth; acquired form develops at any age depending on underlying cause
keyStats.globalCases: Exact global prevalence uncertain; fewer than 1 in 10,000 people are estimated to have congenital tritanomaly

definition: Tritanomaly (ICD-10: H53.55) is a form of anomalous trichromacy in which all three cone types are present but the short-wavelength (S-cone, or blue cone) contains an altered opsin photopigment with a shifted spectral sensitivity. This distinguishes it from tritanopia (H53.54), in which the S-cone is entirely absent, producing dichromacy and a more severe blue-yellow confusion. In tritanomaly, the shift in S-cone peak absorption typically moves toward longer wavelengths, reducing the spectral separation between the S-cone and the medium-wavelength (M-cone), which normally allows fine discrimination of blue and yellow tones. The genetic basis is a heterozygous or homozygous variant in OPN1SW on chromosome 7p13. Autosomal dominant inheritance means a single copy of the variant may be sufficient to produce the condition; autosomal recessive inheritance of more severe variants produces tritanopia. Tritanomaly falls under the ophthalmology specialty for formal diagnosis and is relevant to optometry for colour vision testing. Acquired tritanomaly — a clinically distinct entity coded separately — arises secondary to conditions including age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, optic neuritis, and toxic exposures such as alcohol or styrene; in acquired forms, the deficiency is typically unilateral or asymmetric, whereas congenital forms are bilateral and symmetric.

typesClassification.description: Present from birth due to an OPN1SW gene variant; bilateral, symmetric, and stable throughout life. Milder than tritanopia, with partial blue-yellow discrimination retained.
Develops secondary to retinal disease, optic nerve pathology, or toxic exposure; often unilateral or asymmetric and may fluctuate with the underlying disease course.
Complete absence of functional S-cones producing dichromacy; a distinct condition but classified in the same ICD-10 cluster and often confused with severe tritanomaly.

primarySymptoms: Difficulty distinguishing blue from green — affected individuals may perceive blue objects as greenish, the most characteristic perceptual error of tritanomaly.
Confusion between yellow and violet or grey — yellows desaturate toward neutral grey or take on a violet cast, making these two hues unreliable.
Pastel colours appearing washed out, grey, or indistinguishable — a general loss of saturation for the blue-yellow axis of colour space.
Difficulty reading colour-coded information that relies on blue-yellow distinctions, such as certain maps, safety warnings, bus route displays, and data charts.
Confusion between a light blue and white — the most commonly reported everyday nuisance in mild tritanomaly.
Problems identifying the ripeness of certain foods, particularly bananas (green to yellow transition) and plums (yellow to purple transition).
Normal or near-normal red-green discrimination — affected individuals can typically distinguish red from green accurately, as their M- and L-cones function normally.
Difficulty seeing blue writing on a black board or white background under poor lighting, since contrast in the blue channel is reduced.
Confusion between navy blue and black in clothing and décor — a common practical complaint in daily life.
In acquired forms, a subjective change in the appearance of colours compared with the other eye or compared with the recent past, which is an important symptom pointing to secondary causes.
earlyWarningSigns: A child consistently matching blue and green items together as though they are the same colour.
Difficulty with art projects or colouring tasks that involve distinguishing blue, green, and yellow.
Complaints that a yellow or orange object looks grey or pale in dim lighting.
In adults, noticing that one eye sees colours differently from the other — a key sign of an acquired, potentially treatable cause.
emergencySigns: Sudden onset of colour vision change in one eye accompanied by central visual loss or a visual field defect, suggesting acute optic neuritis or retinal vascular occlusion requiring urgent assessment.
Rapidly progressive bilateral colour vision loss with reduced visual acuity, which may indicate a toxic neuropathy, nutritional deficiency, or compressive lesion needing same-day imaging.
New colour vision asymmetry in a person with known diabetes or glaucoma, potentially indicating worsening retinal or optic nerve disease requiring prompt review.

causes.description: Pathogenic variants in OPN1SW on chromosome 7 alter the amino acid sequence of the short-wavelength opsin, shifting its peak spectral sensitivity toward longer wavelengths and reducing the spectral gap between S- and M-cones that normally allows blue-yellow discrimination.

Drusen accumulation and retinal pigment epithelium atrophy in the macula selectively damage the high-density region of S-cones, producing an acquired blue-yellow deficiency that may appear years before clinically visible structural damage.

Elevated intraocular pressure damages retinal ganglion cells and the optic nerve, with selective early vulnerability of the koniocellular pathway that carries S-cone signals, producing acquired blue-yellow colour vision loss that may precede detectable visual field loss on perimetry.

Microangiopathic damage to the inner retinal layers disrupts the foveal and perifoveal S-cone circuitry; blue-yellow deficiency is among the earliest detectable functional changes in diabetic eye disease, correlating with retinopathy severity.

Inflammation of the optic nerve in multiple sclerosis or isolated optic neuritis preferentially impairs colour contrast sensitivity along the blue-yellow axis, often before visual acuity is significantly affected, and may persist as a subtle residual deficit after acute recovery.

Chronic ethanol exposure, styrene, and certain medications including digoxin, ethambutol, and sildenafil (phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors) are known to produce acquired blue-yellow colour vision deficiency through retinal or optic nerve toxicity, often reversible if detected early.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of tritanomaly or blue-yellow colour vision deficiency
riskFactors.description: OPN1SW variants segregate in families with autosomal dominant or recessive patterns; having a first-degree relative with the condition raises the probability significantly, though precise penetrance data are limited due to the rarity of the congenital form.

riskFactors.factor: Age over 50 with pre-existing macular or optic nerve disease
riskFactors.description: Acquired blue-yellow colour deficiency is strongly associated with aging retinal conditions including age-related macular degeneration and open-angle glaucoma; cumulative photochemical damage to S-cones occurs over decades.

Poorly controlled diabetes accelerates microvascular damage to the retina; colour vision screening in diabetic patients detects early S-cone dysfunction before fundoscopic changes are apparent, motivating tighter glycaemic control.

riskFactors.factor: Elevated intraocular pressure or glaucoma diagnosis
riskFactors.description: Glaucoma-induced pressure on the optic nerve preferentially injures S-cone pathways; early detection and treatment of raised IOP may slow or prevent acquired blue-yellow colour loss.
Long-term heavy alcohol consumption and occupational exposure to styrene or other organic solvents are established causes of acquired colour vision deficiency, including the tritanopic axis.

A personal or family history of multiple sclerosis carries significant risk of optic neuritis, which characteristically impairs blue-yellow colour discrimination even after apparent recovery of visual acuity.

affectedDemographics: Males and females equally for the congenital form, unlike X-linked red-green deficiencies that predominantly affect males
Adults over 50 with age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, or diabetic retinopathy for the acquired form
Individuals with a family history of OPN1SW variants (autosomal dominant kindreds)
Workers with chronic occupational exposure to styrene, toluene, or other organic solvents
People with multiple sclerosis or a history of optic neuritis
Patients on long-term digoxin, ethambutol, or high-dose phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of tritanomaly relies on formal psychophysical colour vision testing rather than self-report, as affected individuals often adapt to their perceptual world and do not recognise their deficiency. The Farnsworth-Munsell 100-hue test and the Farnsworth D-15 panel are the primary clinical tools; both assess arrangement of colour chips along the full hue circle and characterise the axis and severity of any deficiency. Tritanomaly produces a characteristic confusion axis along the blue-yellow direction (tritan axis) on these tests. The Ishihara pseudoisochromatic plates, widely used for red-green screening, contain no tritan-axis plates and will miss tritanomaly entirely. The City University Colour Vision Test and the Lanthony desaturated D-15 panel are more sensitive for detecting mild acquired blue-yellow deficiency. Anomaloscopy — the gold standard for classifying anomalous trichromacy — uses a Rayleigh match (for red-green defects) and a blue-green match for tritanomaly; the blue-green (Moreland) equation reveals the abnormal S-cone tuning. Electroretinography (ERG) with short-wavelength isolating stimuli can provide objective evidence of S-cone dysfunction and is useful in young children or non-cooperative patients. Genetic testing for OPN1SW variants confirms the congenital diagnosis, particularly when autosomal dominant inheritance is being counselled. For suspected acquired tritanomaly, the full ophthalmic workup includes visual acuity, intraocular pressure measurement, dilated fundoscopy, optical coherence tomography (OCT) of the macula and optic nerve, and visual field testing, directed at identifying the underlying retinal or optic nerve pathology.

diagnosticTests.test: Farnsworth D-15 or Farnsworth-Munsell 100-hue panel test
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the axis and severity of colour deficiency by assessing how a patient arranges colour chips; tritanomaly produces a blue-yellow (tritan) confusion pattern.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Under standardised illumination you arrange 15 or 100 coloured discs in order from one reference colour; the test takes 5-20 minutes and is painless.

diagnosticTests.test: City University Colour Vision Test (3rd edition)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for acquired colour deficiency including tritanomaly using a series of reference and comparison colour patches; more sensitive to mild acquired defects than the D-15.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You view a series of cards under standard lighting and point to the colour patch most similar to the central reference; takes approximately five minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The gold standard for characterising tritanomaly; the blue-green equation quantifies the shift in S-cone spectral sensitivity by measuring the ratio of blue to green light needed to match a cyan reference.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You look into an instrument's eyepiece and adjust two fields of light until they appear identical; it is painless but requires concentration and takes 15-30 minutes.

diagnosticTests.test: Short-wavelength automated perimetry (SWAP) or multifocal ERG
diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures S-cone function objectively; SWAP detects glaucomatous damage to the koniocellular pathway earlier than standard white-on-white perimetry.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: SWAP involves pressing a button when you see a yellow target on a blue background in a bowl-shaped machine; electroretinography requires electrodes placed near the eye.

diagnosticTests.test: Optical coherence tomography (OCT) of macula and optic nerve
diagnosticTests.purpose: In acquired cases, OCT identifies structural retinal or optic nerve damage that explains the colour deficiency and directs management of the underlying condition.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A camera scans the back of your eye with a harmless infrared light beam; no contact is required and the scan takes about five minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the molecular basis of congenital tritanomaly and guides genetic counselling for affected families.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample is sent for targeted gene sequencing; results typically take two to four weeks.

treatmentOverview: There is no treatment that corrects the underlying photopigment abnormality in congenital tritanomaly — the genetic defect is permanent, and the S-cones cannot be repaired or replaced with current technology. Management is therefore centred on accurate diagnosis, professional reassurance, and practical adaptations that allow affected individuals to navigate environments where colour coding matters. Specific chromatic filter lenses, including blue-yellow filtering spectacles and tinted contact lenses, have been marketed for colour deficiency but lack robust evidence of clinical benefit for tritanomaly specifically; red-green filter lenses such as EnChroma are designed for the far more common protanopia and deuteranomaly and have no role in blue-yellow deficiency. For acquired tritanomaly, management is directed at the underlying disease: controlling intraocular pressure in glaucoma, optimising glycaemic control in diabetic retinopathy, administering corticosteroids or disease-modifying therapy in optic neuritis, discontinuing or adjusting the offending drug in toxic cases, and treating macular disease with anti-VEGF agents where indicated. Early detection of acquired tritanomaly through routine colour vision testing in high-risk populations — diabetics, glaucoma suspects, and patients on potentially retinotoxic drugs — allows treatment of the underlying condition before more significant visual function is lost. Occupational counselling and disclosure to relevant employers, academic institutions, and licensing authorities is an important component of management for congenital cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Treatment of underlying cause in acquired tritanomaly: IOP-lowering therapy
medicalTreatments.description: Prostaglandin analogue, beta-blocker, or carbonic anhydrase inhibitor eye drops, or laser trabeculoplasty, to lower intraocular pressure in glaucoma; this slows progressive S-cone pathway damage and may stabilise or modestly improve colour vision.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reducing IOP by approximately 20-30% from baseline slows glaucomatous progression in approximately 70-80% of patients in major trials (OHTS, EMGT).

medicalTreatments.name: Anti-VEGF intravitreal injections for macular disease
medicalTreatments.description: Ranibizumab, aflibercept, or bevacizumab injections into the vitreous halt neovascular AMD progression; by preserving macular photoreceptors including S-cones, they can stabilise or partially recover acquired colour vision deficiency.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Approximately 90% of patients with neovascular AMD stabilise vision with anti-VEGF therapy; around 30-40% show visual gain of 3 or more lines in major trials.

medicalTreatments.name: Corticosteroid therapy for optic neuritis
medicalTreatments.description: Intravenous methylprednisolone 1 g daily for three days accelerates recovery from acute optic neuritis, including colour vision recovery, in multiple sclerosis and isolated optic neuritis, though final visual outcome is similar to untreated cases.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Speeds recovery by approximately two weeks compared with placebo (ONTT); approximately 95% of patients recover visual acuity to 6/12 or better at one year.

medicalTreatments.name: Discontinuation of retinotoxic medications
medicalTreatments.description: Stopping or dose-reducing ethambutol, digoxin, or phosphodiesterase-5 inhibitors when they are identified as the cause of acquired colour vision deficiency; recovery of colour discrimination is possible if the drug is stopped early before irreversible retinal damage occurs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Colour vision recovery occurs in approximately 40-60% of cases of ethambutol-induced optic neuropathy when the drug is stopped at first sign of colour deficiency.

medicalTreatments.name: Occupational and vocational adaptation counselling
medicalTreatments.description: Liaison with occupational health, educational psychologists, and driving licensing authorities to document the nature and severity of the colour deficiency, clarify which tasks are affected, and arrange workplace or academic adjustments for colour-reliant duties.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Structured occupational guidance significantly improves reported quality of life and task performance in studies of colour deficient individuals across multiple professional settings.

alternativeTreatments.name: Chromatic spectral filter lenses
alternativeTreatments.description: Tinted spectacle lenses or contact lenses designed to shift or separate overlapping hue sensations have been explored for colour deficiency. For blue-yellow deficiency specifically, there is no commercially validated filter lens with published clinical efficacy comparable to the red-green filter evidence base; users should approach marketed products with caution.

alternativeTreatments.name: Assistive colour-identification technology
alternativeTreatments.description: Smartphone applications such as Seeing AI, Be My Eyes, and specialised colour-detection apps use the phone camera to identify and name colours aloud, providing practical real-time colour recognition assistance that bypasses the perceptual deficiency.

alternativeTreatments.name: Environmental and display adaptations
alternativeTreatments.description: Enabling colour-blind accessibility modes on digital devices, requesting colour-accessible document formatting from educators and employers, and using high-contrast or pattern-coded alternatives to colour coding in professional and educational settings are well-supported adaptive strategies.

whySeeSpecialist: An ophthalmologist should be consulted for formal characterisation of tritanomaly using anomaloscopy or the Farnsworth-Munsell 100-hue test, which requires specialist equipment not available in routine optometry. Specialist assessment is essential when tritanomaly is suspected to be acquired rather than congenital — a distinction with major implications for the need to investigate and treat an underlying retinal or optic nerve condition. Individuals with congenital tritanomaly who are pursuing occupational roles with colour vision requirements (aviation, marine navigation, rail transport, electrical work) need formal documentation from an ophthalmologist for licensing purposes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for an ophthalmologist or neuro-ophthalmologist with a specific interest in colour vision disorders and access to anomaloscopy equipment. For suspected acquired tritanomaly, a retina specialist (vitreoretinal surgeon) is appropriate when macular disease is suspected, and a glaucoma specialist when elevated intraocular pressure is the likely cause. Academic or tertiary ophthalmology centres are most likely to have both the diagnostic tools and the expertise to manage rare congenital colour deficiency alongside acquired causes.
preventionStrategies: For congenital tritanomaly, there is no prevention; genetic counselling is appropriate for affected individuals planning families, particularly in autosomal dominant kindreds.
For acquired tritanomaly, maintain tight glycaemic control if you have diabetes — colour vision testing in diabetic clinics can detect early retinal damage before it progresses.
Have intraocular pressure checked regularly after the age of 40, particularly with a family history of glaucoma; early IOP control protects S-cone pathways.
If you are prescribed ethambutol, digoxin, or long-term phosphodiesterase inhibitors, ask your clinician about baseline and periodic colour vision monitoring.
Limit chronic heavy alcohol intake and occupational solvent exposure, both established causes of toxic acquired colour vision deficiency.
Report any sudden unilateral change in colour perception to an ophthalmologist promptly — optic neuritis causing colour vision change can be an early sign of multiple sclerosis.
lifestyleModifications: Use smartphone colour identification apps as a practical tool for distinguishing clothes, traffic signals, product labels, and food ripeness.
Label clothing with colour tags or organise your wardrobe by brightness (dark to light) rather than by hue.
Request colour-accessible formatting for important documents — pattern, texture, or number labels alongside colour coding are legally required reasonable adjustments in many workplaces.
Ensure good lighting at home and at work; dim lighting exacerbates blue-yellow confusion as the blue channel is disproportionately noise-limited at low light levels.
Use high-contrast themes on digital devices (e.g. dark mode or a yellow-black high-contrast theme) to reduce reliance on blue-yellow colour distinctions.
Inform relevant professionals — your optometrist, driving instructor, aviation medical examiner, or employer — about your colour vision status so safety implications are properly assessed.

recommended: Antioxidant-rich foods — leafy greens, berries, nuts, and fish rich in omega-3 fatty acids — support retinal health and may slow age-related S-cone degeneration
Lutein and zeaxanthin from kale, spinach, and eggs, which are concentrated in the macula and protect photoreceptors from oxidative damage
Vitamin A-rich foods (sweet potato, carrots, leafy greens) to support photoreceptor outer segment renewal
avoid: Chronic heavy alcohol consumption, which is a direct cause of toxic optic neuropathy and acquired colour vision deficiency
Diets lacking in antioxidants and retinal-protective micronutrients, particularly in individuals at risk of macular degeneration or diabetic retinopathy

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic exercise at moderate intensity is associated with reduced intraocular pressure (by approximately 20-25% during exercise and modestly in the longer term) and improved retinal vascular perfusion, both of which may protect against acquired colour vision deterioration in glaucoma and diabetic retinopathy. There are no exercise restrictions specific to congenital tritanomaly. Outdoor sports that require precise colour discrimination — such as certain ball sports, orienteering, or safety-critical activities — should be approached with awareness of the specific hue confusions involved.
dailyManagement: Download and learn a colour identification smartphone app (Seeing AI, Colour ID) to use whenever colour accuracy matters.
Arrange and label your wardrobe systematically to avoid blue-green and yellow-grey clothing confusion.
If you drive, familiarise yourself with the positional layout of traffic signals — in most countries, red is always top and green always bottom, so position rather than colour alone can guide action.
Keep regular appointments with your ophthalmologist if you have glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, or another acquired cause — annual colour vision monitoring tracks disease progression.
Disclose your colour vision status on relevant licensing, employment, or educational application forms where required — non-disclosure creates safety and legal risks.
If symptoms or colour perception change in one eye only, seek an ophthalmology appointment promptly rather than waiting for the next routine review.
prognosis: Congenital tritanomaly is a stable, lifelong condition that does not worsen or improve; the perceptual deficiency remains constant throughout life and does not cause structural eye damage, reduced visual acuity, or progressive visual loss. The condition is compatible with normal education, driving (in most jurisdictions, colour vision is not a statutory requirement for standard car licences), and most careers, with the exception of those with mandatory colour vision standards such as commercial aviation, merchant marine, and rail signals roles. Acquired tritanomaly has a variable prognosis determined by the underlying cause: glaucoma-related colour loss may be stabilised with IOP reduction but rarely fully reverses; optic neuritis-related colour deficiency partially or fully recovers in the majority of patients; and toxic forms can recover significantly if the causative agent is withdrawn early.
recoveryTimeline: For congenital tritanomaly, there is no recovery timeline because the condition is permanent. For optic neuritis, colour vision begins to improve 4-6 weeks after the acute attack and reaches its final level at approximately six months. Ethambutol-induced colour vision deficiency can improve over months to years after drug cessation, though recovery is incomplete in around 40% of cases. Colour vision changes from elevated IOP in glaucoma do not typically reverse after IOP normalisation, but progression halts. Anti-VEGF treatment for neovascular AMD stabilises colour loss within weeks of initiating therapy in responsive eyes.
complications: Occupational restriction — congenital tritanomaly disqualifies individuals from colour-critical roles in aviation (CAA/FAA Class 1 medical), merchant navy, and rail signalling; early formal diagnosis and career counselling are important.

Diagnostic delay for acquired causes — because tritanomaly is unfamiliar to non-specialist clinicians and standard Ishihara screening misses it entirely, an underlying retinal or optic nerve condition may progress undetected.

Psychosocial impact — though rarely disabling, undiagnosed tritanomaly can cause repeated failures in colour-based educational assessments, unexplained workplace errors, and social embarrassment; formal diagnosis resolves uncertainty and enables adaptations.
Misclassification as red-green deficiency — using Ishihara plates alone may lead to a misleading 'normal' result, missing the blue-yellow axis defect and failing to identify an acquired cause.

supportResources.name: Colour Blind Awareness — Types of Colour Blindness
supportResources.description: UK charity with clear patient information on tritanomaly, other colour vision deficiencies, and practical guidance for affected individuals and employers.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Color Blindness
supportResources.description: Patient-focused overview of colour vision testing, types of colour deficiency, and management from the AAO.

supportResources.name: Royal National Institute of Blind People (RNIB) — Colour vision deficiency
supportResources.description: UK resource covering everyday strategies, employment guidance, and assistive technology for people with colour vision deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Deuteranomaly
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Deuteranomaly is a far more common red-green deficiency (affects about 5% of males) caused by an M-cone opsin variant; it does not impair blue-yellow discrimination and is detected by Ishihara plates, which miss tritanomaly.

confusedWithConditions.name: Achromatopsia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achromatopsia is total colour blindness from absent or non-functional cone photoreceptors, also causing poor visual acuity and severe photophobia; tritanomaly spares acuity and produces only a partial blue-yellow axis deficiency.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acquired color vision deficiency
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acquired colour vision deficiency can mimic any axis of deficiency including the tritan axis but is distinguished from congenital tritanomaly by being asymmetric between eyes, associated with ophthalmic disease, and potentially reversible.

relatedConditions.name: Deuteranomaly
relatedConditions.relevance: Another form of anomalous trichromacy but affecting the green-red axis; understanding both helps clinicians choose the correct colour vision test and counsel on occupational implications.

relatedConditions.name: Achromatopsia
relatedConditions.relevance: The most severe form of colour vision loss; both conditions affect the S-cone (blue) system, making achromatopsia an important differential for severe tritanomaly.

relatedConditions.name: Age-related macular degeneration
relatedConditions.relevance: A leading cause of acquired tritanomaly; blue-yellow colour testing can detect early macular S-cone dysfunction before structural changes are visible.

relatedConditions.name: Acquired color vision deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: The parent diagnostic category for secondary forms of tritanomaly; distinguishing acquired from congenital drives the entire investigation and management pathway.

faqs.question: What is tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Tritanomaly is a rare colour vision deficiency affecting the blue cone photoreceptors. The blue cone is present but contains an altered photopigment, reducing the ability to distinguish blue from green and yellow from grey or violet.

faqs.question: How common is tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Congenital tritanomaly is extremely rare — estimated to affect 0.0001 to 0.002% of the population. Acquired blue-yellow colour deficiency from retinal or optic nerve disease is considerably more common, though exact prevalence data are limited.

faqs.question: Is tritanomaly the same as blue-green colour blindness?
faqs.answer: Tritanomaly specifically impairs the blue-yellow axis of colour vision. Affected individuals confuse blue with green and yellow with grey or violet. It is distinct from red-green colour blindness, which is far more common and affects different cone types.

faqs.question: Does tritanomaly affect men more than women?
faqs.answer: No. Unlike red-green colour blindness, which is X-linked and affects males far more often, tritanomaly is caused by a gene on chromosome 7 and is inherited in autosomal fashion, affecting males and females equally.

faqs.question: How is tritanomaly diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires colour vision tests that specifically assess the blue-yellow axis, such as the Farnsworth D-15 panel, the City University test, or anomaloscopy. The standard Ishihara plates used in routine screenings do not detect tritanomaly.

faqs.question: Can tritanomaly be treated or cured?
faqs.answer: Congenital tritanomaly cannot be cured; the genetic change in the blue cone photopigment is permanent. Management focuses on adaptive strategies, assistive technology, and occupational adjustments. Acquired forms may partially improve when the underlying cause is treated.

faqs.question: What colours does tritanomaly affect?
faqs.answer: Tritanomaly impairs the blue-yellow axis of colour space. Blue objects may appear green, yellow may appear grey or violet, and pastel blues and whites may be indistinguishable. Red-green discrimination is typically normal.

faqs.question: Can colour filter glasses help tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: There are no validated chromatic filter lenses specifically designed for blue-yellow colour deficiency. Red-green filters such as EnChroma are not designed for the tritan axis and would not help. Assistive apps for colour identification are a more practical option.

faqs.question: Can children have tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Yes. Congenital tritanomaly is present from birth. Children may be identified during school colour vision screenings, though standard Ishihara-based screening misses it. Parents who notice a child consistently confusing blue and green should request a specialist colour vision assessment.

faqs.question: Is tritanomaly inherited?
faqs.answer: Congenital tritanomaly is inherited through variants in the OPN1SW gene on chromosome 7. It can follow autosomal dominant or recessive inheritance patterns. A parent with tritanomaly may pass the variant to children of either sex.

faqs.question: Does tritanomaly affect driving?
faqs.answer: Most standard car driving licences do not require specific colour vision standards; position and brightness of traffic lights allow colour-deficient drivers to identify them. Commercial driving, aviation, and marine licences have stricter colour vision requirements that may be affected.

faqs.question: Can I develop tritanomaly later in life?
faqs.answer: Yes. Acquired tritanomaly can develop at any age as a consequence of age-related macular degeneration, glaucoma, diabetic retinopathy, optic neuritis, or drug toxicity. New or worsening blue-yellow colour confusion in an adult warrants an ophthalmology assessment.

faqs.question: What careers are affected by tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Commercial pilots, merchant navy officers, and rail signal workers have mandatory colour vision standards that tritanomaly may fail. Electricians, graphic designers, and some laboratory roles also rely on accurate colour discrimination. Early career counselling after formal diagnosis is advised.

faqs.question: How does tritanomaly differ from tritanopia?
faqs.answer: In tritanomaly the blue cone is present but contains an altered photopigment, preserving partial blue-yellow discrimination. In tritanopia the blue cone is entirely absent, producing complete loss of blue-yellow discrimination (dichromacy) and more severe confusion between affected colours.

faqs.question: What assistive technology helps with tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Smartphone colour identification apps such as Seeing AI and Colour ID can name colours aloud in real time. Accessibility modes on phones and computers can replace colour-only coding with patterns or labels. These tools help manage daily colour-dependent tasks effectively.

faqs.question: Does glaucoma cause tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Glaucoma damages optic nerve fibres that carry signals from blue (S) cones particularly early in the disease course. This produces an acquired blue-yellow colour deficiency detectable before significant visual field loss on standard perimetry, making colour testing a useful glaucoma-monitoring tool.

faqs.question: Will tritanomaly get worse over time?
faqs.answer: Congenital tritanomaly is stable for life and does not worsen. Acquired tritanomaly may progress if its underlying cause — such as glaucoma or diabetic retinopathy — is not treated. With treatment of the underlying disease, acquired blue-yellow colour loss can stabilise or partially recover.

faqs.question: Should I tell my employer about tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: Disclosing a colour vision deficiency to employers is advisable for roles where colour accuracy is safety-critical. In many countries, employers are legally required to make reasonable adjustments once informed. Non-disclosure in safety-critical roles can create legal and safety risks.

faqs.question: Is the Ishihara test useful for diagnosing tritanomaly?
faqs.answer: No. The Ishihara pseudoisochromatic plates are designed to detect red-green deficiencies and contain no tritan-axis plates. A person with tritanomaly will typically pass an Ishihara test and receive a false-normal result. Blue-yellow-specific tests are required.

faqs.question: Can diabetes cause blue-yellow colour vision problems?
faqs.answer: Yes. Diabetic retinopathy can damage the foveal and perifoveal photoreceptors that carry blue cone signals. Blue-yellow colour deficiency is among the earliest detectable functional changes in diabetic eye disease and correlates with retinopathy severity.

metaDescription: Tritanomaly is a rare blue-yellow colour vision deficiency affecting males and females equally. Learn causes, diagnosis, and management. Doctor-reviewed.
